data.title: BrainsWay: Strong Growth Prospects Matter Little To The Market
data.description:
BrainsWay's (NASDAQ:BWAY) Q4 financial performance was extremely strong and I was surprised by the company's aggressive growth target for 2025. Despite this, the stock continues to tread water, due in part to a rising share count and flat margins.

I previously suggested that a higher valuation and increased growth investments would likely lead to weaker returns, which has proven to be the case so far. While a strong argument can be made that BrainsWay's share price should be higher, I continue to believe that the company's margins will drive returns, meaning the stock could be in for a period of stagnation. BrainsWay's business is also highly sensitive to the business cycle, meaning there is growing downside risk at the moment.

For investors taking a longer term view, BrainsWay appears to be establishing a strong position in the TMS market and current R&D investments could provide upside in the future. I also expect BrainsWay's operating margin to trend towards 20% as the business scales.

BrainsWay Business Updates

With its financials strengthening significantly over the past 12-18 months, BrainsWay is shifting its focus from profitability to growth. Much of this is related to clinical trials that will hopefully expand the company's addressable market in the future.

BrainsWay plans on conducting clinical trials with its Deep TMS 360 system (rotating magnetic field), with the first trial planned for the second half of 2025. This is likely to be followed by feasibility studies in areas like Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's. While success in either of these areas would be enormous, TMS has already been investigated in these types of areas before and demonstrated marginal efficacy.

BrainsWay continues to advance its clinical trial evaluating an accelerated MDD treatment protocol in the US. This basically refers to compressing treatments into a period of a few days rather than over the course of weeks. The company is in the final stage of recruitment and plans on providing news about this indication in the third quarter.

BrainsWay also recently announced positive data from a study assessing Deep TMS as a treatment for alcohol use disorder. The company believes this data is supportive of conducting further research into TMS as a treatment for AUD. I find it difficult to get excited about this indication though given the cost of TMS and time commitment required of patients. BrainsWay received FDA clearance for Deep TMS as a smoking cessation aid several years ago and yet there has been no word on the commercialization or insurance reimbursement front for some time, suggesting this initiative has been quietly shelved.

Other initiatives include:

  • Collection of data to support treatment of adolescent depression patients
  • Expanding reimbursement for the treatment of PTSD in Israel
  • Assessing the efficacy of Deep TMS in combination with psychedelics for treating depression

On the commercial front, BrainsWay's success continues to be driven by its focus on larger enterprise customers. Demand for OCD is also reportedly supporting growth at the moment and is expected to remain a driver of sales in 2025. Some of this is likely the result of increased coverage, with close to 100 million people now covered in the US. BrainsWay is not unique in being able to offer TMS for OCD though, suggesting other factors are also at play.

Financial Analysis

BrainsWay generated $11.4 million of revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 27% YoY. Revenue was well ahead of my Q4 expectations and was driven by extremely strong system sales. BrainsWay shipped 75 systems in Q4, up 25% YoY, continuing the company's recent strength relative to competitors like Neuronetics (STIM).

BrainsWay expects $49-$51 million of revenue in 2025, implying 20-24% YoY growth. This guidance is strong and again, was well ahead of my expectations. BrainsWay also suggested on its Q4 earnings call that its backlog is growing and that it has maintained a positive book-to-bill ratio in recent quarters. This is a new data point that suggests current growth is sustainable and doesn't just represent the company rapidly working its way through its pipeline.

BrainsWay's operating profit totaled $430,000 in the fourth quarter at a 3.8% margin. Adjusted EBITDA was $1.4 million at a 12% margin. While BrainsWay's margins are continuing to move higher, recent gains have been fairly modest, which has likely been driven by an increased focus on growth.

BrainsWay was profitable and cash flow positive throughout 2024 and guidance suggests this situation is likely to continue in 2025. The company has suggested a 3-4% operating margin and an 11-12% adjusted EBITDA margin for the full year, meaning margins will be fairly flat YoY.

BrainsWay had close to $70 million cash at the end of 2024, with the sequential increase largely driven by Valor Equity Partners' investment. I remain puzzled as to why existing investors were diluted and what this capital is needed for though. BrainsWay initially suggested that the additional cash would help it to explore new markets, revenue channels, and commercial partnerships. Even with increased investments in 2025, the company is still likely to be cash flow positive. BrainsWay has also suggested that it is focused on organic growth, seemingly ruling out any acquisitions.

Conclusion

BrainsWay's valuation is still reasonable given that it is a profitable, high-growth medtech company with a strong competitive position. The company's forward EV/EBITDA ratio is only around 20x, and the company has a long growth runway and plenty of room for further margin expansion.

This matters little at the moment though. BrainsWay's share price has been driven by cash flows in recent years, meaning returns are likely to be weaker while margins aren’t rapidly improving. I also believe that a lot of the upside has been taken off the table if the company is not going to return capital to shareholders at a depressed valuation.

Consideration also needs to be given to the fact that BrainsWay’s business has proven sensitive to the business cycle in the past, meaning there is significant downside risk in the event of a recession. BrainsWay's large cash balance means that it is well positioned to weather any downturn, but this won't necessarily stop its stock from declining at least 30-40% from current levels in the event of a recession.

data.title: Mastech Digital Is Valued Like A Turnaround, Now It Needs To Earn It
data.description:
Thesis

Mastech Digital, Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:MHH), a U.S. company that offers IT staffing and digital transformation services, has for quite a while worked in the space where older workforce solutions meet newer technology consulting. Lately, though, it’s been putting more focus, gradually but steadily, on building up its data and analytics capabilities.

I initiated coverage on the company with a “Hold” rating last September, after it had gone through a stretch of underperformance, there was a drop in revenue and a noticeable slowdown in project activity. That said, it did have a solid balance sheet at the time, and there were some early, if cautious, signs that its more profitable areas were starting to pick up again.

Since then, Mastech has shown clear improvement in margins and overall profitability. Even so, it’s still very much in a period of strategic transition under new leadership. There’s no forward guidance to speak of, and progress on growing revenue has been fairly limited. So, while the operational gains are certainly worth noting, my view is that MHH is priced about right for where it stands, a company with real potential, but one that hasn’t yet demonstrated the steady growth, reliable execution, or clear strategy needed to support a stronger rating.

Strong Quarterly Gains

Mastech Digital’s Q4 2024 revenue for the quarter reached $50.75 million, up 10.2% year-over-year, a solid headline number underpinned by a 26.2% jump in Data and Analytics Services revenue and 6.8% growth in IT Staffing. The Data and Analytics segment in particular stood out, climbing to $10.3 million and demonstrating not just year-over-year strength but sequential momentum as well, up 9.4% from Q3.

Gross margins also impressed, reaching a company record of 29% in Q4. Both business segments contributed. Data and Analytics Services posted a 480 basis point expansion to 49.5%, approaching its internal target of 50%, while IT Staffing improved by 350 basis points, aided by an uptick in direct hire activity and lower healthcare costs. The result was a non-GAAP net income of $2.8 million [$0.23 per share], nearly doubling the prior year’s $1.3 million, and a return to GAAP profitability: $0.3 million compared to a loss of $5.4 million in Q4 2023.

Based on my perspective, these figures indicate that the company has momentarily reversed its negative operational patterns. The annual results support the turnaround story as gross margins improved to 27.9% in 2024 from 25.4% in 2023. GAAP EPS shifted from a $0.61 loss to a positive $0.28 figure while non-GAAP EPS increased to $0.71 marking a 61% improvement compared to the previous year.

But that’s just one side of the story.

Demand Weakness Lingers

Despite the margin wins, it’s impossible to ignore the undercurrent of demand-side fragility and execution risk that came through on the earnings call. Case in point, CFO Jack Cronin acknowledged that “order bookings in Q4 2024 totaled $11 million, which was somewhat below our expectations.” He went on to note that project delays, particularly in the Data and Analytics segment, were driven by a “little bit of uncertainty in the marketplace.”

The implication is that while margins are recovering, the top-line growth story is not yet secure. Client hesitancy, longer decision cycles, and an unpredictable macro backdrop continue to weigh on near-term momentum. And it’s not just anecdotal. Q4 revenue missed consensus estimates by $1.21 million, a notable gap for a company with a sub-$120 million market cap.

Compounding this concern is the seasonal headcount reduction in IT Staffing: 63 billable consultants rolled off projects in Q4. While the company framed this as typical year-end behavior, it’s still a material contraction that will likely show up in Q1 2025 revenue softness.

No Strategic Visibility

Equally important is the vacuum in forward guidance. CEO Nirav Patel, just 45 days fresh into his tenure at the time of the call, emphasized that he is still “listening and learning” and taking “a structured approach in defining our long-term strategy.”

In other words, Mastech isn’t ready to articulate a roadmap yet, not for growth, not for acquisitions, and not for product direction.

For investors, I'd say that leaves a dangerous blind spot. So without a clear strategy or measurable targets, the company's improving margins feel less like the beginning of a durable inflection and more like a defensive maneuver in a still-fragile environment.

To be fair, Patel’s comments did strike a confident tone around Mastech’s position in the AI-led transformation space. “I have no doubt that the AI revolution is real,” he said, adding that companies are “rapidly scaling these capabilities to drive competitive advantage.”

At this point, the whole “AI revolution” storyline should be familiar to just about anyone who’s been paying attention to the market over the past few years. It’s starting to sound less like insight and more like something lifted straight from a template.

In reality, the challenge for Mastech, though, will be proving it can capture that opportunity in a scalable, differentiated way. As of now, its bookings shortfall and flat full-year revenue, $198.9 million in both 2023 and 2024, indicate it hasn’t yet figured that part out.

From a capital position, Mastech remains healthy. It closed the year with $27.7 million in cash and no bank debt, offering dry powder for future investment. It also extended its share buyback program through 2026, with 423,000 shares remaining authorized.

But cash is only as valuable as the opportunities a company can effectively deploy it into. And right now, with M&A on pause and strategic initiatives still under development, that liquidity isn’t translating into immediate value creation.

Valuation and Growth Potential

As I see it, Mastech Digital’s valuation is starting to look more like a discounted bet on what might happen, rather than a premium price for what already has.

At 13.72 times blended P/E, the stock does look inexpensive, especially when you consider it drops to 9.81 times based on the 2026 earnings estimate. That sort of multiple usually suggests one of two things: either the market has overlooked something, or it sees a real risk in how the company will execute going forward.

In Mastech’s case, I tend to think it’s the latter.

The lack of near-term growth clarity is what ultimately undermines my investment thesis, especially when juxtaposed against the optimism baked into 2026 earnings forecasts. I think that optimism might be mathematically justifiable on paper, but it’s not yet evident in the company’s operations or strategic direction.

In the last earnings call, Patel acknowledged as much, stating plainly, “I am taking a fairly structured approach in defining this strategy,” while adding that he plans to share specifics “sometime later [in] the second half of this year.” That vagueness isn’t unusual for a new CEO, but for me, it’s problematic for valuation, especially for a stock that depends on near-term inflection to justify even modest rerating.

Margins vs. Cash Flow

One important concern that may have slipped under the radar is the growing gap between Mastech’s margin performance and its actual cash generation. The 29% gross margin in Q4 looks good on the surface, no question there. But at the same time, operating cash flow fell 55% from the year before, and levered free cash flow dropped by 66.6%. That kind of split suggests that the gains in margin aren’t yet showing up in how efficiently the company runs or how well it uses its capital.

On the call, CFO Jack Cronin didn’t sugarcoat this either. While proud of margin improvements, he provided no commentary on capital allocation beyond maintaining current SG&A levels and keeping CapEx minimal, less than $1 million for the year.

That conservative posture speaks volumes. A company truly leaning into growth doesn’t hunker down on spend; it scales selectively. Mastech, in contrast, appears to be playing defense with its cash position despite a clean balance sheet and $27.7 million in liquidity.

Revenue Stuck in Place

The muted topline adds to this narrative of strategic limbo. Despite strength in Data and Analytics Services we covered earlier, which rose 26.2% year-over-year, the overall revenue figure for 2024 was unchanged. That’s not a rounding error, it’s a sign that the business, as currently structured, lacks growth momentum. Cronin pointed to a few project delays and some general market uncertainty as the reasons the company came up short. And while that may very well be the case, it doesn’t do much to reassure me that Mastech is prepared to manage things now that demand is starting to return to a more typical pace.

The issue of customer concentration looms large in this context.

As Nirav Patel himself noted, Mastech needs to transition into “an AI-first organization” with differentiated offerings, but the roadmap for that transition is still vague. In the meantime, the firm remains heavily exposed to a handful of large clients in its staffing segment, a point of vulnerability that could unravel revenue stability further if even one of those relationships is disrupted. And that’s not a hypothetical concern. The Q4 reduction of billable consultants was framed as seasonal, but it highlights how fragile staffing utilization can be in a labor-sensitive model. The ripple effect is real, particularly as it threatens to weigh on Q1 2025 results.

Final Verdict

I’m maintaining my "Hold" rating for MHH. In any valuation framework, the question is always: what are you paying for, and what are you actually getting? With MHH, investors are paying for a company that looks cheap relative to its earnings power two years out, but they’re getting a business that has no forward guidance, no near-term revenue acceleration, and a leadership team still in the midst of discovery mode.

Unless and until we see an articulated strategy tied to measurable KPIs and a return to top-line growth, I can’t endorse the idea that MHH is fundamentally mispriced. The discount is real, but so is the uncertainty. This isn’t a margin story anymore. It’s a trust story, and right now, I'm not buying it.

data.title: Golden Ocean Group: Only Time Will Tell The Effect Of Tariffs, Uncertainty Prevails
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to look at Golden Ocean Group Limited’s (NASDAQ:GOGL) 2024 performance in more detail and give some comments on the potential uncertainty in 2025 and what is keeping me on the sidelines. The overarching theme of tariff war is prevalent throughout the stock market. The company may see a further slowdown in operations, but given its strong financial position, will weather the storm with relative ease in my opinion.

Briefly on performance

Starting from the top, we can see that in the last quarter of the year, the company experienced a decline in revenue. The decline can be attributed to lower TCE rates and fleet utilization, as well as the decrease in on-hire days and fewer operating days in general. There has been a decline in dry bulk freight rates in the fourth quarter driven by softening demand in select commodity groups such as essential Agri bulks like wheat, corn, and soybeans. Overall, because of the previous quarters, the company achieved a respectable y/y growth of around 10% even with the rather disappointing Q4.

Moving on to the company’s efficiency and profitability, which shows us a similar story. The company was enjoying an improvement across the board for the first three quarters of the year and giving some of it back at the end of the year. This stemmed from the same issues as above, as well as higher drydocking expenses and overall higher operational costs. Drydocking expenses skyrocketed to $34.3m compared to $9.7m in Q3. Overall, though, the company did improve its efficiency and profitability for the year by around 300bps across the board.

Regarding some other important metrics, GOGL’s ROA and ROE have somewhat recovered since the end of 2023; however, they are still quite low and could be improved upon, especially when comparing them to its peers. We can see below that the company is somewhere at the bottom of the comparison, but not too much, which means such returns are sector-wide phenomena. In terms of competitive advantage, we can see many companies are unable to surpass 10% ROTC, which tells me that it may be hard to do in this sector, apart from ZIM, which seems to be an outlier. Overall, the management is probably doing the best it can to utilize the company’s assets and shareholder capital; however, there could be some improvements.

Now, let’s look at the company’s financial situation. GOGL finished the year with around $129m in cash and equivalents, as well as $1.2B in long-term debt. How worrisome is this gap? That depends on the company’s operations and cash flow. There are a couple of metrics I like to look at to assess the risk of debt. Firstly, I like to look at the company’s operations at the end of the year and what they pay out in interest on the debt outstanding. The company’s operating income came in at $314m, while it paid out $108m in interest expenses. This means that its interest coverage ratio is around 3x.

Many analysts believe that anything over 2x is healthy. I like to see at least 5x, but that’s just me being more conservative. Secondly, its operating cash flow was around $365m, which also easily covers its interest expense on the debt outstanding. And thirdly, its debt-to-equity is around 0.62, which indicates a balanced capital structure, and it seems to be below the maritime industry average, which is around 0.76.

The interest rate on the debt outstanding is rather high, so the payments are quite high too. Any further increases in interest rates leave the company rather exposed; however, we may have seen the end of the interest rate hikes for now, and maybe we should expect USD SOFR rates to come down in the long run.

Overall, I don’t see any liquidity risk here. The company generates decent cash flow, and its operations are profitable, which should help it whether any sort of downturn that we may experience going forward.

Comments on the Outlook

Speaking of a potential downturn... The company expects further declines in TCE rates for at least the first quarter of 2025. The projected TCE rates are $15,100 for Capesize vessels and $9,900 for Panamaxes. That is a significant reduction even from Q4 ’24, not to mention Q3 ’24, which most likely led to the company cutting its dividend by half. I don’t think this is going to be fixed in the next couple of quarters due to the uncertainty in the economy overall. Many analysts believe that the trade war will be a net negative for many industries, including dry bulk carriers such as GOGL. This will bring a lot more volatility in TCE rates and demand, which will be very hard to predict going forward, mainly because we do not know what the new Trump Administration has in store.

The imposition of tariffs on China and many other countries could disrupt the well-established trade routes, leading to a shift in cargo flows. The Trump administration is going to announce the new Liberation Day plan at 4 pm ET, which will be reciprocal tariffs, like 25% on Canada and Mexico, as well as tariffs on lumber, copper, pharma, and microchips. All this tariff uncertainty is bringing a lot of volatility to the stock markets, with wild swings from deep in the red to green in a matter of hours.

We still do not know what the end plan is of this administration, but recession fears due to tariffs are rising, and I do believe that there will not be a winner in the end, despite what Trump insists upon. This will be a net-negative, although, I would like to be proven wrong. Below is an overall idea of what the administration already has in effect and what might be still in store going forward.

Grain exports of key dry bulk commodities like corn, and wheat may decline because of retaliatory measures and higher costs due to tariffs is certain. It will be interesting to hear what the management will have to say in the next quarterly conference call, which is scheduled in May, and whether they will address the tariff issue this time around. I would say this will be a huge talking point for analysts on the conference call. Also, I wouldn’t be surprised if we get further updates on the company’s outlook, which most likely will be to the downside.

Closing Comments

There is too much uncertainty surrounding Golden Ocean Group Limited, and given that the next quarter seems to be even worse, I don’t think this is the right time to jump in just yet. Even after such a decline in share price, around 50% from recent highs, it still seems that the bottom is not there yet given the ongoing tariff uncertainty and overall elevated volatility. Trump’s Liberation Day announcement will be just the beginning of another back-and-forth flip-flopping show, which means volatility will remain elevated for a lot longer, which already seems to be going on for well over a month.

I am going to be paying attention over the next few months to gauge the sentiment of the investing community and whether that sentiment turns positive regarding the proposed tariffs. What I will be looking for is that the fear of tariffs was overblown all along, and we can return to an uptrend, however, that remains to be seen.

Nevertheless, the company isn’t going anywhere anytime soon. Positive cashflows and profitable operations should help it weather this uncertainty. I just don’t think it’s the right time to jump in just yet.

data.title: Alexandra Real Estate: More Problems Brewing, Stay Out
data.description:
On our previous coverage of Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (NYSE:ARE), we were not swayed by the bullish arguments. Despite some interesting valuation metrics, we decided to stay out.

You can argue that the stock is cheap at about 10X FFO. It is. You can argue that it is hard to buy BBB+ rated companies at this multiple. That is true as well. You are also likely to make money from here over the next 10 years. It seems like a mathematical impossibility that your total returns would be negative. But is this a table-pounding buy? We are going to take a pass.

Source: You Just Got Litt Up!

We did not miss much by staying out.

Bulls have been treated to 6 additional "Strong Buys" on Seeking Alpha. We have looked at the recent data and decided that we are actually more concerned today than we were a few months back.

Expanding Our Thesis

We won't repeat the work in the first article, so investors need to read that piece before they get to this. But we are going to be looking at how the data from then, is shaping up today. From everything we have been seeing, the problems are getting worse.

About 42% of the Big Apple's life-sciences stock was vacant in the fourth quarter — more than double the average vacancy rate of 20.5% across 24 other cities.

Source: Biz Journals

Both those numbers look scary. For context, ARE's vacancy for New York and other major locations is shown below.

Boston, which is the biggest hub for ARE and the US as a whole, is experiencing an absolute collapse in occupancies.

“Vacancies continue to rise with each passing quarter. The total availability rate of 26.9%—one of the highest in the nation—is 10 percentage points higher than availability one year ago,” says the Colliers report. “New construction was a major contributor to the increased vacant space, adding pressure to an already softened market.”

Key Takeaways from the Report:

Increased Vacancy: In the fourth quarter of 2024 alone, over one million square feet of space were delivered to the market, much of it remaining empty. This surge in new supply further amplified the already-high vacancy rates.

Negative Net Absorption: For the first time in more than a decade, the Boston life sciences market saw negative net absorption during the fourth quarter of 2024, pulling the annual total into the red. This marks a significant shift, as demand for space has not kept pace with the increasing supply.

Surplus of Available Space: Currently, there are over 15 million square feet of available life sciences space in the Boston area, a concerning figure for landlords and developers.

Source: Boston Real Estate Times (March 12, 2025)

We can see some other markets in the picture below.

We think San Francisco Bay area is the more vulnerable as both its lab vacancy and office space vacancy are close to 30%. We also have massive conversions planned there, further increasing pressure on lab space rents.

We always bet on some level of convergence over time. Yes, ARE's properties are better and the Mega Campus model is more resilient. But lab space is fungible. If there are huge vacant spaces, landlords will genuflect to get the tenants in. This will pressure either rents or occupancy or both on ARE's side. Already, we have seen ARE's guidance for 2025 ending occupancy to be the lowest in two decades. We are predicting this will get worse. While ARE's leases are well spread out, the sheer vacancy levels in its major footholds will force rents and occupancies lower.

One minor positive here is that the new lab space (this does not count conversions) is tapering off in a hurry.

While this is good, it is likely to be a positive beyond 2027 at the earliest. Currently, we don't need a single new square foot built, and well, we are still building.

Demand Side

While the supply side looks bad, the demand side is about to get horrific. The new administration is bringing the hammer down on NIH funding.

The scope of the cuts

The Trump administration announced last month that it is capping indirect costs for NIH research grants at 15%. This change does not affect NIH funding for direct costs, which refer to researchers’ salaries and laboratory supplies. Instead, the policy shift decreases the funds available for indirect expenses like utilities, equipment, security and legal compliance.

Before this shift, universities and other research institutions negotiated an indirect cost rate with the federal government, and it usually was about 50-60% of direct research costs — so this change will hurt. The move is projected to cut billions of dollars from research aimed at developing cures and treatments for diseases like cancer, Alzheimer’s, diabetes, mental health disorders and opioid abuse.

Source: Med City News

Anyone thinking this does not destroy the long case for lab space is hallucinating. The total dollars moving lower means less personnel, less lab size and/or lower rents.

We have not even gone to the persistent targeting of the pharma industry, which has been an ongoing issue since day 1.

This takes one of the most promising technology out of grants. The same technology that yielded breakthrough results against pancreatic cancer, one of the hardest to treat malignancies. This should be absolutely riveting for new US research.

Finally, there is this.

Maybe the actual people leaving will be less. But we are at a state of supply-demand for Lab space, that we really cannot afford anyone leaving. Whether you believe in the actions of the new administration or not, is rather irrelevant. What is relevant is that it will drive ARE out of business.

Verdict

Forget the low FFO multiple. Forget the BBB+ rating. There is no bargain here. There are hurricane sized winds coming your way. We doubt the investment grade rating survives 3 years from now. NAV estimates are trending down, as they should. Last time the mean estimate was near $140/share and we are down to $135 today. Our estimate of realistic liquidation value is under $110/share and moving lower as well. We would stay out and use any rallies to exit. We tend to use "sell" ratings only for stocks we are ready to short-sell. This does not fit that at present, so we are going with a "hold" for now.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: Ford Vs. GM: Which One Will Be Impacted More By Tariffs?
data.description:
TARIFF... the word of the year so far as we are a quarter of the way through 2025. To some, it inspires fear. To others, it is "a beautiful word, the best word." To me, it is a great way to exercise my flexible investing muscles. That means looking at the full landscape of how the US Administration's plans, and how other countries respond, as perhaps the most opportunistic time for all-weather investors (like me) in many years.

The auto industry is just one of many that will be impacted. However, it might be the one that feels it the most, at least initially. Cars are manufactured in many countries, and much of that involves cross-border activity on the way to the finished product. So the two legacy US auto makers, Ford (NYSE:F) and General Motors (NYSE:GM) are a topic any tariff-following investor should be tracking closely now. I am not a big "car guy" or car stock guy. But like us all, I like to make money and not lose it. So, let's hop in the front seat and hit the gas pedal on this one... even if not all of it is actually gas anymore.

F and GM are expected to bear the brunt of 25% tariffs taking effect this month. Let's look first at the impacts on the industry as a whole.

The most obvious impact is that it is expected to put pressure on the prices of imported cars, which in turn should help domestic automakers a lot by reducing competition from Foreign brands such as BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), NIO (NIO), Honda (HMC), Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) and others. The car industry is very sensitive to the macroeconomic scenario, be it tariffs, pressure on household income and so on, so this increase in prices could keep this market more stagnant for a while.

It may seem easy to estimate the impacts, since it's obvious that carmakers who import more vehicles from outside the US will be directly impacted more than others, but that's not all. Even American automakers use parts from abroad, such as cheaper components from China and Mexico. Therefore, by extending tariffs to auto parts, this increases logistical complexity and creates a higher cost in the supply chain, i.e. indirectly raising costs and putting pressure on margins even for automakers who are not heavily importing the vehicles.

Therefore, in the medium term, even American automakers that seem like they will benefit at first - like Ford - may also suffer negative impacts.

General Motors: The Underdog

General Motors has facilities in Mexico and Canada, with analysts estimating that around 49% of vehicles are imported. This is a lot. For a company that is already not having an easy time growing top-line, being badly affected by competition in markets outside the US, this tariff not only makes prospects much more uncertain, but could also be a new detractor for short-term fundamentals.

Obviously, sales can be affected by this, but as already mentioned, the additional costs can also put pressure on the margin, especially in the first quarters where there may be attempts to adapt to this new environment.

Another thing we have to look at is the composition of these automakers' revenues. Below is GM's vehicle sales table.

Note that although most of its sales occur in the United States (2.7 million), the company is also very exposed to markets such as China. As if the market there wasn't difficult enough (note how GM has been losing market share in China year after year), there is now also a chance of retaliation from these tariffs, which would probably reduce GM's share on China even further.

Ford: a relatively better scenario vs. GM

For Ford, the scenario is much less cloudy. Its domestic footprint is stronger when compared to General Motors and there is some data that suggest as much 80% of the vehicles sold by Ford in the U.S. are assembled in America. But note that the word used is assembled. This is because some of the components also come from other countries such as Mexico.

Even so, the picture is much clearer than that of General Motors. The impact of retaliation on Ford is much smaller, since most of its sales take place in the US, as the table below shows.

Tariffs Add More Uncertainty

As mentioned at the beginning of the article, at some point this may benefit some car companies by mitigating competition, but in the end, it's not exactly good for Ford's shareholders. At the end of the day, the level of uncertainty is higher, and the market doesn't like uncertainty.

But in trying to “price” these uncertainties, it's clear which one the market sees in a better light, and this becomes clear after looking at the companies' sales and manufacturing data.

While GM is trading at a forward price-to-earnings of 4.2x, Ford's multiple sits at 7.5x. Historically, Ford has a premium over GM, but in the past the gap wasn't so wide. Note that while the average P/E for F stock was 7.9x, GM's was 6.8x. Now the difference is almost 80%, so with a change in the news (which frankly, is not impossible given the recent volatility on this kind of news), this gap could close.

So, I see that even though the picture is much clearer for Ford, the valuation ends up balancing it out a bit, but still, it's a no-go for both due to the unpredictability of the industry at the moment and other structural factors such as cyclicality, high competition and disruption that we're seeing in the market.

So to me, the question we all prioritize... what to do about these stocks, if anything... comes down to what I'd consider a classic "tiebreaker" for my investing style. To summarize so far:

  • Impact will be felt
  • Companies are not flush with success riding into this, neither are they reminiscent of the near-collapse of the industry earlier this century.
  • Markets are keyed up (pun unintentional) for "something" to happen.
  • The Seeking Alpha quant grades (below) as well as our own work indicate that Ford might have an edge in terms of valuation.
  • However, at a charged time like this (dang it, another unintentional pun!) my bottom line is the price chart.

Technicals are the tiebreaker for me

Not always to say "buy or sell" but to assess the overall odds of each stock going up, say 10%, before it drops 10%. Because no matter what fundamental work anyone assigns, these stocks are geopolitical footballs now. That's even more than a plain old political football! So, what do the charts say.

GM chart: nothing interesting to say

For those keeping score at home, that's a very obscure dad joke-style quote from a old animated movie called "A Bug's Life." Compared to F, which as you'll see excites me a bit in one respect, GM is like the animated stick bug in the movie (played by the guy who was also the brother in the TV show "Frasier"). He's just sitting there, with nothing interesting to say, feeling sorry for himself. Like this chart of GM.

I see a lack of inertia in both directions, and while the all-time high is 40% north of here, it will take a real-world catalyst to get it moving. Could that be a relief rally that lasts, as the market "sells the rumor, buys the news" on GM and the tariff thing in general? That's my best guess, though I put a low probability on it. GM has is nearly doubled since the lows of late 2023, so it will take a lot to make it a buy for me. Hold? OK, I can settle for that, given the middling picture here. But watch that PPO indicator at the bottom. It is figuring out where to send GM stock next.

F Chart

Oops, that subhead sounded awkward, sorry. Ford is clearly the opportunity to me, technically speaking. There's always a lot on my desk when it comes to what to buy next, if anything. But my often-discussed use of option collars is quite relevant here. That's where I buy stocks to try to hold them for years, but avoid the frequent "earnings bombs" and in this case, "tariffs-crashing sales bomb" that is possible with F.

So above, I see a chart that has a few things I like. Not all the time, but in this market environment, where I believe contrarian investing, not momentum investing, is the better path if holding more than 12 months is the objective. Momentum and shorter-term trading time frames go together, in my book.

Ford has been in the stock market's garage (finally an intended automobile pun!) for years. The tariffs may be poised to arrive at 25% or higher, but this stock is "on sale" at 60% off its early 2022 high. That is, maybe the bad news is out. The lower part of the chart shows that there is at least some percolating upside hints there. This and the GM chart above are 3-day price bars, which I like to use since they are in between the commonly-used daily and weekly charts. Always trying to find an edge.

Ford a buy (but read carefully for how), GM a hold

So for "ratings" purposes, I will place a buy on F and a hold on GM. But now, how to express that buy of Ford in my portfolio? I'm very risk averse.

Ford stock is in a downtrend for sure. I looked into my usual go-to in this market, a Ford option collar. That's where for every 100 shares of stock I buy, I also buy a put option and in most cases, also sell a covered call option. This secures a worst case outcome from the start, which is particular helpful when a stock is in play like this, with many external forces determining its price, not simply fundamental analysis. There are literally hundreds of combinations for a liquid stock like this.

However, I could not find one I liked for now, mostly because I could not get the big upside I wanted without taking on more downside than I wish (10% or less, usually much less). So instead I "took a shot" which I'm always fine with doing, with small amounts of my capital.

The trade with Ford: small call option buy

I decided to move ahead today in my Sungarden YARP Portfolio and buy a small call option position on Ford. I went out less than three months, since I think this stock is going to move one way or the other soon. I didn't buy shares of stock, but for every $33 I spend, I get the right to buy 100 shares of Ford at $11.02. If it flies to $11.33 by June 20, I break even. If it rockets to $12 by then, I've tripled my money. Most importantly to me, I can't lose more than what I put up. I'll take those odds.

Remember what I said at the top: flexibility. That is not specific to auto stocks, but to every decision I make in 2025 and likely beyond. We've had tariffs for decades, but never quite like this, in a time of instant communication and heavy popular interest in the stock market. Washington DC is playing by a new set of rules, so should we.

data.title: Coca-Cola's Iconic Campaign Returns, But It May Not Be Enough To Overcome Slow Growth
data.description:
Thesis

Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO) is bringing back one of its most iconic marketing campaigns, which could have an even bigger reach and impact this time. Despite being a market leader in many markets, it still has a lot of room to grow, particularly in developing markets where commercialization is still expanding. While there is a rise in health consciousness, Coca-Cola remains well-positioned Coca-Cola with a wide range of healthy options. On the other side, it is facing many challenges such as currency headwinds, the rise of private label brands, and it is losing its moat with the rise of e-commerce. Even though Coca-Cola is well-positioned and has room for growth, current challenges support a 'Hold' rating.

Coca-Cola is bringing back one of its most iconic marketing campaigns

Share a Coke was a genius idea that Coca-Cola introduced in 2011. It made a positive impact on Coca-Cola's brand and created free word-of-mouth marketing on a global scale. As stated on Coca-Cola's website, "Starting this year in April, consumers will begin finding Coca‑Cola cans or bottles with names on store shelves. The rolling global launch will ultimately include 120+ countries." Fifteen years have passed since they released it the first time, and bringing it back will encourage older audiences to tap into nostalgia while also offering a first time experience to newer generations. There was also a trend of sharing photos of bottles over social media. Sharing a picture of a bottle with your friends’ names was a creative way to show them that you are thinking about them. With social media being much bigger now, I think that it can help this campaign have an even greater reach and impact. I also expect that this campaign will have lower costs than the last time, since they are already familiar with the processes and technology needed to create it.

Strategic position for the rising health trend

Despite the growing health trend, I believe Coca-Cola will be less affected than many think. This trend is mostly present in developed countries, where only a part of the population is starting to care more about health and sugar intake. More than 80% of the global population does not live in developed countries and is much less impacted by this trend. Additionally, the largest share of Coca-Cola’s revenue comes from emerging markets, which are growing faster than developed markets. Consumers who are following this trend still have many options, from the most similar product to original Coca-Cola, Coca-Cola Zero, to Minute Maid and even to various water brands owned by Coca-Cola like Dasani, Ciel and Smartwater. With all these options, I believe that Coca-Cola is effectively hedged against this trend.

Coca-Cola Zero as a strong hedge against health trends

As consumer preferences are evolving as a result of a rise in health consciousness, including concerns regarding caloric intake, there has been a significant rise in demand for zero sugar drinks. While trademark Coca-Cola revenue grew only by 2% for the full year 2024, driven by growth in Latin America, Asia Pacific and North America. Coca-Cola Zero grew by astonishing 9%. I believe that Coca-Cola Zero is extremely well positioned to take the zero sugar industry. It is profiting from the rise of usage of zero sugar drinks, while also profiting from well-known brand names. Its competitor, PepsiCo, also introduced Pepsi Zero Sugar, but it has been outperformed by Coca-Cola Zero by market share and consumer preference. While Coca-Cola and Pepsi have historically close rivalry in the regular soda market, I believe that the zero-sugar market will continue to be dominated by Coca-Cola Zero.

Coca-Cola has significant room to grow as commercialization expands in emerging markets

There is a huge growth opportunity in developing and emerging markets, where 80% of the world’s population lives and where natality rates are highest. In those markets, only 32% of beverages are commercially sold, compared to 70% in developed markets. As those markets continue developing, Coca-Cola will benefit from commercialization due to its globally known brand and unique business model. Unlike its competitors, Coca-Cola has spun off its bottling operations. By separating bottling from its core business, Coca-Cola has reduced operational complexity, making it easier for them to expand into new markets.

Valuation

Coca-Cola is one of the very few companies that kept their spot in the top 100 biggest US companies by market cap from 1955 until now, while more than 90% of companies that were once ranked in the top 100 have since fallen out of that list. This demonstrates that Coca-Cola is one of the very few companies that has proven itself indestructible, even in times of uncertainty.

Coca-Cola has spent decades building a moat by, as stated in Charlie Munger's talk, "creating and maintaining conditioned reflexes, consistently associating the brand with things people admire like youth and happiness. It has been a part of countless celebrations and moments throughout consumers' lifetimes, building a deep, emotional connection," which new brands can’t replicate. This gives Coca-Cola a strong advantage over newer beverages and makes its high valuation more justifiable.

Compared to its competitors’ gross margins, Dr Pepper at 56%, PepsiCo at 55% and Monster at 54%, Coca-Cola has a higher gross margin of 61%. This advantage is partially attributed to its spinoff of bottling operations. By separating bottling from the core business, Coca-Cola is in a situation where it can reduce operational complexity and, by doing so, have even more focus on its brand than its competitors.

On the other hand, the company expects to deliver organic revenue growth of 5 to 6 percent, but it expects 3 to 4 percent currency headwinds, resulting in expected revenue growth of 1 to 3 percent for 2025, which is significantly low for such a high multiple.

Over the past 16 quarters, Coca-Cola has traded at an average Price to Earnings multiple of 25x. Currently, it trades at a multiple of 29x, which is above its historical levels and seems high for a mature company that is not growing that fast. Compared to its competitors, PepsiCo, Dr Pepper and Monster, who trade at P/E ratio of 21.5x, 32x and 38x respectively, and who also have similar capital structures as Coca-Cola, resulting in an average P/E ratio of 30.5x. Applying this ratio of 30.5x, which is lower than Monster's ratio, as Monster is growing faster than Coca-Cola, but higher than PepsiCo’s, since PepsiCo is growing slower than Coca-Cola, it would imply a stock price of $73, which is slightly above the current price of $71. Therefore, based on its strong durability and competitive advantages, offset by slower expected growth due to currency headwind and historically high P/E ratio, the upside remains limited, making the stock a "Hold" at its current valuation.

Risks

Many of Coca-Cola's products are impulse buy products. Coca-Cola has spent years positioning its products in order to make them easily accessible for impulsive purchases. Coca-Cola also has leverage over small retailers and can often require favorable product placement. With the rise of e-commerce, they risk losing these advantages.

Increases in the value of the U.S. dollar against other currencies could affect Coca-Cola’s future earnings. Based on the current rates, Coca-Cola anticipates an approximate 3% to 4% currency headwind, but there is a risk that currency headwinds exceed expectations.

Aspartame, an ingredient that is contained in Coca-Cola Zero, is widely approved by health authorities, but there are ongoing research activities that are evaluating its long-term effects. There is a slight risk that future findings may reveal risks associated with consuming Coca-Cola Zero, which would modestly slow down its sales.

In most developed markets, there is rising competition from retailers that have developed their own private-label beverages that are similar to Coca-Cola. Walmart, Lidl, Aldi and other retailers sell their own private-label cola up to 50% lower than Coca-Cola. In times of recession, this could hurt Coca-Cola's market share as consumers turn to cheaper alternatives.

Conclusion

Coca-Cola still has a lot of room to grow, mostly in developing markets where commercialization is still expanding. However, with slower expected growth due to currency headwind and historically high P/E ratio, the upside remains limited, making the stock a 'Hold' at its current valuation.

data.title: Crinetics Pharma: Strong Progress With Multiple Indications And Upcoming PDUFA
data.description:
I covered Crinetics Pharma (NASDAQ:CRNX) back in 2023, when it was developing Paltusotine for acromegaly in a phase 3 trial. Now, they have positive data and a September 25 PDUFA date, so let us take a look.

Background

Lead asset Paltusotine is an oral, selective, non-peptide, somatostatin receptor type 2 (SST2) agonist. An alternative to injectable broad spectrum somatostatin analogs, paltusotine is a non-peptide small molecule that only targets SST2, one of the five somatostatin receptor subtypes. This makes it different from traditional somatostatin analogs (e.g., octreotide, lanreotide), which are peptide-based biologies. Activating SST2 receptors may lead to effects such as inhibition of hormone secretion (e.g., growth hormone, insulin, glucagon) and tumor growth suppression in neuroendocrine tumors (NETs). As I noted before:

In acromegaly, currently available peptide drugs need painful monthly or daily injections, or a strict twice-a-day oral dosing regimen. Current somatostatin peptide drugs often fail to halt disease progression.

Current standard of care in acromegaly is resection, however, surgery alone does not always help normalize GH and IGF levels. Radiation and adjuvant pharmacotherapy - somatostatin analogues, dopamine agonists and growth hormone antagonists - are needed to normalize those hormones.

Paltusotine is an oral SRL (Somatostatin Receptor Ligands) like Mycapssa, however, and the company claims its small molecule nature gives it certain advantages. Mycapssa is oral octreotide, and while non-inferiority trials have been positive, in practice, oral octreotide is generally considered of superior efficacy if more difficult to administer. In a phase 2 study called ACROBAT, which had both Paltusotine and other oral SRLs, data were confusing because of the non-randomized nature of the trial, and then generally loose trial design. So, an OLE study was performed, which “saw that paltusotine lowered and maintained IGF-1 at levels comparable to prior injected SRL therapy for up to 103 weeks, and that at Week 52, thirty-two (88.9%) of the 36 respondents preferred once daily paltusotine treatment.” However, we still needed a thorough and well-designed trial.

The two phase 3 PATHFINDER studies tested paltusotine’s ability to reduce IGF levels in two patient populations - one octreotide-treated group, and the other treatment-naïve.

Data

Data from the PATHFINDER-1 study was announced in September 2023, and from the PATHFINDER-2 study in March 2024. In both studies, paltusotine met the primary and all secondary endpoints. Below, I put the data side by side in a single table.

What we see here is that, unsurprisingly, pretreated patients did better, numerically, than treatment naive patients. We can see that they had a higher IGF-1 control rate (83% vs. 56%), suggesting that those who were already stable on injectables transitioned better to paltusotine. They had better GH suppression (87% vs. 57%), demonstrating that moving from injectables preserved hormonal control better. Interestingly, the untreated group had a larger IGF-1 reduction, but we have to understand that this was simply because they started at a higher baseline IGF-1, rather than showing better efficacy. Pretreated patients started in control and remained stable, therefore their IGF-1 reduction range seems low, but is not actually so.

If you compare this data with the mycapssa Optimal trial where the defined IGF-1 levels range was similar to paltusotine, we see that the former saw ~60% of patients maintained IGF-I response vs ~20% of patients on placebo; while paltusotine had 83% of participants maintained IGF-1 levels compared to 4% on placebo. Placebo-adjusted data for paltusotine was much superior.

Jefferies analysts see a $300mn opportunity in acromegaly for the company.

Other Updates

Last year, paltusotine saw a win in a second indication, carcinoid syndrome, where a phase 2 trial demonstrated “rapid and sustained reductions in flushing episodes and bowel movement.” Key data:

  • 63% reduction in mean flushing frequency for patients with >1/day at baseline (n=24; p<0.0001)
  • 60% reduction in mean excess BM frequency (defined as daily bowel movements above the upper limit of normal, 3/day) in patients with >3/day at baseline (n=16; p=0.02)
  • 61% reduction in mean flushing severity (n=31; p<0.0001) and 64% reduction in mean BM urgency (n=31; p<0.0001)
  • Reductions in frequency and severity of symptoms were observed within 2 weeks of paltusotine treatment and sustained through 8 weeks in both naïve/untreated patients and those switching from prior somatostatin receptor ligand (SRL') therapy

Around 12,000 of Americans are diagnosed with neuroendocrine tumors every year. 20% of these, or roughly 2400 patients, have carcinoid syndrome. Injectable SRLs are standardly used for the disease, however, like with acromegaly, they have strong side effects.

A second phase 2 trial for atumelnant in congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH') also reported positive data in January, although the stock traded “sharply lower” on the data. Data saw the molecule produce ““rapid, substantial, and sustained” reductions in A4 levels with a statistically significant effect across all atumelnant doses after 12 weeks of therapy.” The company plans a global phase 3 trial in the indication, a genetic disease characterized by excessive A4 levels.

According to a source:

Classic CAH affects as many as 1 in every 10,000 to 15,000 people living in the United States and Europe. Nonclassic CAH affects about 1 in every 100 to 200 people. Both classic and nonclassic CAH affect people worldwide.

Apart from hormone replacement and symptom control using glucocorticoids, Crenessity from Neurocrine (NBIX), approved in December, is the key rival. In trials, data was as follows:

The group that received Crenessity reduced their daily glucocorticoid dose by 27% while maintaining control of androstenedione levels, compared to a 10% daily glucocorticoid dose reduction in the group that received placebo.

Also:

At week 4, androstenedione levels decreased with crinecerfont (-299 ng per deciliter) but increased with placebo (45.5 ng per deciliter) (least-squares mean difference, -345 ng per deciliter; P<0.001).

While baseline A4 levels were different, Atumelnant achieved much higher reductions.

Financials

CRNX has a market cap of $3.12bn and a cash balance of $1.4bn. Research and development expenses were $66.6 million for the three months ended December 31, 2024, while general and administrative expenses were $28.2 million. Their CFO for the quarter was ($64.7mn) while capex was ($1.0mn), which gives them an FCF of -$65.7 and a cash runway of 21 quarters.

Risks

The stock has a sizable 8.35% short interest, which shows not everyone is seeing the rosy picture here. It is also confusing to see the stock going down on releasing positive data for Atumelnant in CAH, possibly due to Crenessity having been approved as a rival just the previous month. Other than that, there’s not a lot to complain about with CRNX, except perhaps the fact that its lead indication targets a smallish market.

Bottom Line

CRNX has a PDUFA in 6 months and the stock is trading quite down right now, presenting an opportunity before the PDUFA. However, for longer-term investors, more clarity on the CAH situation is needed, which happens to be the larger market.

I believe that this company is not currently well-positioned for a sustainable recovery in the future because its sales and demand in China, which has become an increasingly important geographic location for the company's revenue, has been experiencing weakness. Additionally, the company's SG&A margin is significantly higher than that of its competitors, thereby impacting its profitability and pricing competitiveness. Lastly, I compiled the competitive metrics of Shiseido and its competitors and found that its lack of competitive edge has been contributing to its decline in popularity among brands.

Based on the chart, Japan is the largest region where Shiseido’s revenue comes from. However, the share of revenue from Japan has declined significantly from 38.09% in 2019 to only 22.26% in 2022, before recovering to 28.65% in 2024.

Meanwhile, China, which accounted for the second largest revenue share and only slightly trailing behind Japan, has increased its contribution from 19.11% to 25.23% in the same period. Notably, China was once the largest segment of the company back in 2021 and 2022, when Japan was the only region that was experiencing a decline in its revenue. The revenue shares from all other regions remain relatively stable during this period.

In terms of revenue, Japan experienced the largest historical annual average decline of 6.8%. The revenue from this region decreased sharply especially during the COVID-19 years before recovering starting from 2023. On the other hand, revenue from China remained relatively strong throughout the pandemic due to the increase in duty free allowance in China downtown implemented during this period. However, the revenue from China has been experiencing negative growth since 2022 and only a slight 0.8% increase in 2024.

I believe the announcement of the discharge of Fukushima wastewater, which has led to increased tension and boycott of Chinese consumers toward Japanese products, have impacted on the sales of the company from China. Also, according to management, the change in spending pattern of these Chinese consumers has also impacted its Travel Retail Business, which has faced flattish historical average growth of only 0.1%, driven by the sharp declines seen starting in 2023.

The EMEA, which is the third largest region of the company, has seen the highest growth of 3.6% among all other regions. I believe this growth is driven by Shiseido’s focus on expanding physical stores for its UK business, which is evidenced by its retail sales growing by around 20% in 2023.

Also, the company has been addressing the under penetration of fragrance business in its UK market, which only represented 20% of its revenue in the UK. Shiseido has recently signed a long-term fragrance partnership with Max Mara, a European company, which I believe could expand Shiseido’s portfolio in the fragrance market, especially within the EMEA region.

Overall, Japan remains as the region with the largest revenue share of the company, though I believe that China’s significance in the company has been increasing, as evidenced by its share of revenue follows closely to that of the Japan market and has once surpassed Japan to be the largest region of the company revenue. However, the revenue from the China market has been showing weaknesses, which could impact the company’s revenue growth trajectory given its increasing importance.

Profitability Comparison

Next, the performance metrics of Shiseido and its competitors is compiled to identify the trends in their growth and determine if Shiseido’s weak performance is due to overall market weakness or company specific factors.

Image created by author with data from Annual Reports

Shiseido's main competitors include L'Oreal (OTCPK:LRLCY), Estee Lauder (EL), and Coty (COTY). According to the chart, Shiseido’s performance across all metrics is the weakest among competitors except for its gross margin. While the overall market revenue grew by an annual average of 5.8% in the past 5 years, Shiseido’s revenue only grew by 2.1%, significantly underperforming its competitors. Though it has the second highest gross margin of 76%, its operating margin (0.8%) and net margin (-1.1%) are much lower than the competitor’s average of 9% and 4.6% respectively.

Though management attributed the decline in operating and net margins to the non-recurring expenses related to structural reform aimed at regaining growth in Japan, after adding back this expenses of 28.8 billion yen, an operating margin of 3.7% and net margin of 1.8% are still derived, which are still well below that of the competitors.

I also noted that Shiseido has the highest SG&A margin of 75.9%, which is higher than the competitor’s average of 62.1%. I believe this substantially higher SG&A expenses, relative to revenue, contributes to the underperformance of operating profit margin and net profit margin of the company.

In view of this higher-than-peers SG&A margin, management has since 2022 implemented a business strategy called “SHIFT 2025 and Beyond” which includes several structural reform plans and targeted a 15% core operating profit by 2027.

However, I compared the recent years’ performance of the company with the targeted margin and noted that there is a significant shortfall. For instance, the core operating margin, which is expected to increase to 9% in 2024, has fallen from 4.1% from 2023 to 3.7% in 2024 in contrast.

Meanwhile, SG&A expenses excluding non-recurring items, have also continued to increase from 71.2% in 2023 to 73% in 2024. I suppose this indicates that the company hasn’t been demonstrating its effectiveness in addressing the high SG&A margin concern, thereby impacting its profitability margins. Also, I believe that the company’s target for its profitability is unlikely to be achieved by 2027, therefore I think taking a more conservative approach for valuation might be more appropriate.

Competitive Analysis

Lastly, the competitive metrics of Shiseido with its peers is analyzed to determine if Shiseido has any competitive edge that will help it to outperform the market and improve its margins moving forward.

The breadth of the brands portfolio of a company could indicate its ability to cater to the different consumer segments and preferences, while having a stronger geographic presence could reflect the extent to which a company has access to the markets and consumer bases. Meanwhile, having a higher R&D margin may show a company’s commitment to product innovation to address consumer needs, and the number of patents protects a company’s intellectual property resulted the R&D. The Net Promoter Score tracks if the consumer of a brand “would recommend using the product based on a scale of -100 to 100”. A higher score would mean that consumers are more satisfied with the brand, therefore creating more value for the company through popularity among consumers.

According to the ranking, L’Oreal holds the top position by having the greatest number of brands under its portfolio, strongest global geographic presence, and the highest number of patents. This top ranking is also supported by L’Oreal, being the company with the highest profitability and highest revenue growth, as shown in the last section. Meanwhile, Shiseido ranks third among its peers. Notably, it has a high R&D margin, which I suppose resulted in its relatively high number of patents. However, despite having the highest R&D margin, its product portfolio is still less extensive than that of L’Oreal and Coty, which would limit its addressable market as compared to its competitors.

I look into the Google search trends over the past 12 months to determine the popularity of each brand among customers worldwide.

Based on the data provided by Google Trends, the popularity of the Shiseido search term is significantly lower than that of the other three competitors on average. I believe this indicates that the consumer awareness and interest globally for Shiseido may not be as prominent as that of its competitors. This lower visibility might diminish the brand effect of Shiseido, especially in terms of online presence.

Risk: Shift in Consumer Preference in China

The China market accounts for the second largest revenue in the company. In recent years, there has been a shift in consumer preference for buying Japanese goods due to increased diplomatic tension between Japan and China, with the Fukushima waste water incident further deteriorating the trade relationship. Additionally, as noted by management in the latest earnings call, Shiseido’s pricing in the mass market is high relative to the Chinese competitors. Specifically for consumer products brands such as AUPRES, management stated that they are not able to offer “competitive pricing to compete with China’s local brands” for now. I believe this would mean that the Chinese consumers might prefer to purchase cheaper products from the local brands competitors, and if Shiseido's profitability margins may be impacted if it were to lower its prices to maintain its market share in China.

Verdict

Overall, I believe that revenue from China is increasingly more significant to the company’s performance, yet the revenue from this market has been declining due to more intense competition from Chinese local brands as well as the wastewater effect on Chinese consumers’ spending behavior. Also, Shiseido’s SG&A is much higher than that of its competitors and has not been showing any notable improvement since its announcement of strategic reform. Therefore, I believe its peers maintain a competitive edge over Shiseido on cost structure. Moreover, Shiseido only ranks third when its competitive metrics are compared against its competitors. Its popularity among consumers is also the lowest among all three brands, which I believe suggests the lower visibility and interest in Shiseido’s products.

The revenue of each region is projected based on their 3-year average revenue growth and tapered off the growth of 0.5% each year starting from 2026 as I would like to remain conservative about the company’s performance, given that the company does not possess a strong competitive edge in its product offerings.

Image created by author with data from SeekingAlpha

Based on the 5-yr Industry Average EV/EBITDA (19.96x) of 9 cosmetics companies- Estee Lauder (EL), L'Oreal (OTCPK:LRLCY), Coty (COTY), Shiseido (OTCPK:SSDOY), Kose (OTCPK:KOSCF), LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), Procter & Gamble (PG), L'Occitane (OTCPK:LCCTF), and Unilever (UL), and a WACC of 5.4%, a price target of $15.35 is derived, which represents a downside of 18.8%. Hence, I rate the company as a Sell.

Conclusion

Overall, I believe Shiseido’s revenue from its main markets, Japan and China, have been underperforming due to the company’s lack of competitiveness in terms of its product offerings and consumer awareness. On top of the below average revenue growth in the past 5 years, the profitability of the company is also underperforming its peers due to high SG&A and the company’s ineffectiveness in implementing its restructuring plan, which has failed to meet its targeted profitability metrics. I believe this disadvantage in cost structure further limits the company’s competitiveness in the China market, where the local brands are able to offer products to mass market at a comparatively lower price.

data.title: Enigmatig Pursues IPO On Deteriorating Fundamentals
data.description:
Enigmatig’s Revenue Dropping Along With Operating Results

Enigmatig Ltd (EGG) has filed to raise $16.9 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to an SEC F-1 registration statement.

EGG provides licensing services and related regulatory maintenance services to clients internationally.

Topline revenue growth is falling, and financial results are declining.

Given the firm’s declining financial results, exuberant valuation assumptions and numerous risks, my outlook on the EGG IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Enigmatig Do?

Singapore-based Enigmatig Limited was founded to develop a range of corporate licensing and regulatory compliance services primarily aimed at small- and medium-sized businesses in the greater Asia-Pacific region.

Management is headed by founder, Chairman and CEO Mr. Foo Chee Weng Desmond, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2019 and was previously senior associate at Saxo Capital Markets Pte and a co-founder at Blackwell Global Investments.

The company’s primary offerings include the following:

  • Business licenses

  • Brokerage licenses

  • Regulatory consultancy services

  • Corporate secretarial services

As of Sept. 30, 2024, Enigmatig has booked fair market value investment of $117,370 from investors, including the founder and various individuals.

The company pursues clients from among small- and medium-sized companies seeking business, brokerage or other regulatory licensing or compliance services.

In fiscal 2024, the firm counted 55 clients from 15 different jurisdictions.

However, it has significant customer concentration risk, with its two largest customers accounting for 47.9% of its corporate services revenue in the most recent fiscal year.

Payroll & Employee Benefits costs as a function of topline revenue have fallen as revenues have decreased, as the figures below indicate:

Payroll & Employee Benefits

(Source - SEC)

The Payroll & Employee Benefits efficiency multiple, i.e., how much new revenue is generated by each dollar of Payroll & Employee Benefits expense, was negative (1.5x) in the most recent reporting period, a poor result. (Source - SEC)

The firm has little recent activity regarding related party transactions, although previous activity was partially settled via digital asset transfers.

Market Overview For Enigmatig

According to a 2024 market research report by Grand View Research, the Asia-Pacific market for regulatory consulting outsourcing services was an estimated $343.7 million in 2023 and is expected to exceed $676 million by 2030.

This represents a forecast CAGR of 10.2% from 2024 to 2030.

The primary reasons for this expected growth are increasingly complex regulatory compliance requirements, changing supply chain dynamics, and the need for greater automation of licensing and compliance efforts to increase efficiencies.

The company faces an increasingly fragmented and competitive market environment. Its competitors not only include direct competition from other consulting-type companies, but also from law firms and accountancy companies that provide many of the same services as they seek to increase their client "share of wallet."

Also, the chart shown here illustrated the historical and expected growth trajectory for the industry through 2030:

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Enigmatig:

Strengths

  • One-stop service offerings, local presence in major hubs

Weaknesses

  • Customer concentration, limited service diversification

Opportunities

  • Geographic expansion, M&A possibilities, technology investment

Threats

  • Fragmented competition, geopolitical risks, regulatory complexity

Enigmatig’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Declining topline revenue from a small base

  • Reduced gross profit and gross margin

  • Lower operating profit

  • Increasing cash used in operations

IPO Details For Enigmatig

Enigmatig intends to raise $16.9 million in gross proceeds from an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, offering 3.75 million shares at a proposed midpoint price of $4.50 each.

No existing shareholders have indicated an interest in purchasing additional shares at the IPO price.

With a completed IPO, the firm’s enterprise value would be approximately $115 million, not including the effects of underwriter over-allotment options.

The float to outstanding shares ratio (ex-underwriter over-allotments) will be approximately 13.04%.

The company, executives and directors and major shareholders have agreed to a standard 180-day shareholder sale or issuance lock-up period.

Management disclosed it intends to apply the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

approximately 30%, [...] is expected to be used for research and development for software;

approximately 40%, [...] is expected to be used for expansion through strategic acquisitions and investments. As of the date of this prospectus, no acquisition target has been identified;

approximately 25%, [...] is expected to be used for increasing sales and marketing capabilities; and

the balance of the net proceeds for other working capital and general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC)

The company's online roadshow is not available.

Regarding outstanding legal proceedings, the leadership said the company is not presently a party to any administrative or legal proceedings that are likely to have a material adverse effect on its operations or financial condition.

The sole listed bookrunner of the IPO is Prime Number Capital.
